hook: You already know that triangles have three sides and three angles, and you can measure both. But here's something interesting: if I hand you a triangle with sides measuring 5 cm, 5 cm, and 8 cm, or one whose angles are 90°, 45°, and 45°, can you tell me what *type* of triangle it is — and why those types even matter? There's actually a whole classification system for triangles based on their sides and their angles, and without it, you'd struggle to describe a triangle precisely or recognize special properties it might have. What makes one triangle fundamentally different from another?
concept: Classifying triangles means sorting them into named categories based on two independent features: the lengths of their sides and the measures of their angles. By sides, a triangle is *equilateral* (all three sides equal), *isosceles* (exactly two sides equal), or *scalene* (no sides equal). By angles, it is *acute* (all angles less than $90°$), *right* (exactly one angle equal to $90°$), or *obtuse* (exactly one angle greater than $90°$). Every triangle gets one label from each category.
anatomy: A triangle has three sides (the line segments connecting the vertices) and three interior angles (the angles formed at each vertex where two sides meet). When classifying by sides, the key measurements are the side lengths themselves — equal-length sides are called congruent sides, marked with tick marks in diagrams. When classifying by angles, you look at the largest angle: if it equals exactly $90°$ it is called a right angle, if it exceeds $90°$ it is obtuse, and if all three are below $90°$ the triangle is acute. The sum of all three interior angles always equals $180°$, which constrains which combinations of angle-type and side-type are possible.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by reading the given measurements for the triangle. Notice that all three sides are equal and all three angles are equal — this gives us everything we need to apply both classification systems.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Side classification: all three sides are equal} \Rightarrow \textbf{Equilateral}
worked_example.description_latex: Because all three sides share the same length of 6, we call this an equilateral triangle. Remember, equilateral literally means 'equal sides,' and it requires all three to match — not just two.
Every angle is $60°$, which is less than $90°$, so all three angles qualify as acute angles. When every single angle in a triangle is acute, the entire triangle is classified as acute.

We combine both labels to give the complete description. Every triangle can be described this way — one label from the side system and one from the angle system — so 'equilateral acute' fully identifies the type of this triangle.

partial_example.description_latex: Here are the measurements for a new triangle. Let's classify it by sides first: look at the three side lengths 3, 5, and 7 and ask whether any of them are equal to each other.
Since 3, 5, and 7 are all different from one another, this is a scalene triangle. Now look at the angles: $25°$, $60°$, and $95°$. Notice that one of these angles is greater than $90°$.

Based on the angle measures $25°$, $60°$, and $95°$, classify this triangle by its angles. Which angle is greater than $90°$, and what does that tell you about the triangle's angle classification? Write the angle type.

Because $95° > 90°$, there is one obtuse angle, making this an obtuse triangle. Combining both labels, we call it a scalene obtuse triangle — it has no equal sides and one angle wider than a right angle.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A triangle has sides } 4, 4, 4 \text{ and angles } 60°, 60°, 60°. \text{ Classify it by sides and by angles.}
\text{A triangle has sides } 5, 5, 8 \text{ and angles } 40°, 40°, 100°. \text{ Classify it by sides and by angles.}
\text{A triangle has sides } 3, 4, 5 \text{ and angles } 37°, 53°, 90°. \text{ Classify it by sides and by angles.}

\text{Two angles of a triangle measure } 45° \text{ and } 45°. \text{ Find the third angle, then classify the triangle by its angles. The two equal sides have length 7 and the third side has length } 7\sqrt{2}\text{. Classify by sides as well.}

\text{A triangular garden plot has fence lengths of 9 ft, 12 ft, and 15 ft. A surveyor measures one corner and finds it is exactly } 90°\text{. Without measuring the other angles, classify the triangle fully by both sides and angles.}

common_mistakes: Students often classify a triangle as 'isosceles' only when it looks symmetric in a diagram, ignoring the actual side-length numbers. The fix: always base your classification on the given measurements, not the appearance of the figure, because diagrams are not always drawn to scale.

Students sometimes think a right triangle cannot also be isosceles, as if the two systems are linked. In reality, the side classification and angle classification are completely independent — a triangle with two equal sides and one $90°$ angle is correctly called an isosceles right triangle, and there is nothing contradictory about that.

When asked to classify by angles, students check whether any angle equals $90°$ but forget to check if any angle exceeds $90°$, defaulting to 'acute' whenever there is no right angle. The fix: always identify the largest angle first — if it is greater than $90°$ the triangle is obtuse, even if the other two angles are small and acute-looking.
untested_variants: Classifying a triangle given only a description in words (e.g., 'one angle is twice another and the third is $80°$') that requires setting up and solving an equation using the $180°$ angle-sum rule before classifying.
Determining whether a given combination of classifications is possible or impossible — for example, explaining why an equilateral obtuse triangle cannot exist.
Classifying triangles drawn on a coordinate grid where the student must first calculate side lengths using the distance formula before applying the classification rules.
Problems where a triangle is described by only two angle measures and the student must first find the third angle before performing the angle classification.
